lesson.title: Passwords, Managers, 2FA, and Phishing Awareness
lesson.explanation:
Passwords are the most common security mechanism protecting your digital identity, finances, and personal information. Yet password practices remain one of the weakest links in personal cybersecurity. Understanding how passwords are attacked, what makes them strong, and how to use additional protective layers like password managers and two-factor authentication is essential digital literacy.

Passwords are typically attacked through several methods. Brute force attacks try every possible combination systematically. A 4-character numeric PIN (10,000 combinations) can be cracked in seconds. Dictionary attacks use lists of common words, common passwords, and known leaked passwords. The password 'password123' appears in virtually every leaked password database. Credential stuffing uses passwords leaked from one service to try logging into other services, exploiting the common habit of reusing passwords. If your email password was 'Summer2024!' and it was leaked from a breached shopping site, attackers will try that same password on your email, bank, and social media accounts.

Password strength is determined by length and complexity. A 6-character password using only lowercase letters has approximately 300 million combinations and can be cracked in seconds with modern hardware. A 12-character password using mixed case, numbers, and symbols has over 400 trillion combinations and would take centuries to brute force. However, length matters more than complexity: a 20-character passphrase of random words ('correct horse battery staple') is stronger than a short complex password ('P@s5w!') and easier to remember.

Password managers solve the fundamental problem that humans cannot remember unique, strong passwords for 100+ accounts. A password manager is software that generates, stores, and auto-fills strong, unique passwords for every account, secured behind one master password. You remember one strong master password; the manager remembers everything else. Leading options include Bitwarden (open source, free tier), 1Password, and KeePass (offline, open source). Using a password manager is the single most impactful security improvement most people can make.

Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) adds a second verification step beyond your password. Even if an attacker obtains your password, they cannot access your account without the second factor. The most common types are: SMS codes (a code sent to your phone, better than nothing but vulnerable to SIM swapping), authenticator apps (TOTP codes generated by apps like Google Authenticator, Authy, or Aegis, which are much more secure than SMS), and hardware keys (physical devices like YubiKey that you plug in or tap, the most secure option). Enable 2FA on every account that supports it, starting with email and financial accounts.

Phishing is a social engineering attack where an attacker impersonates a trusted entity (your bank, a colleague, a service provider) to trick you into revealing passwords, clicking malicious links, or downloading malware. Phishing emails and messages are increasingly sophisticated, often perfectly mimicking legitimate communications. Red flags include: urgency ('Your account will be closed in 24 hours!'), generic greetings ('Dear Customer' instead of your name), mismatched URLs (hovering over a link reveals a different domain than expected), requests for passwords or personal information (legitimate companies never ask for your password via email), and slightly misspelled domains (g00gle.com instead of google.com).

key_vocabulary.definition: Software that generates, securely stores, and auto-fills strong, unique passwords for all your accounts, protected by a single master password
key_vocabulary.example_usage: After switching to a password manager, she had unique 20-character passwords for all 87 of her online accounts without memorizing any of them.

key_vocabulary.definition: A security method requiring two different types of verification to access an account, typically something you know (password) and something you have (phone or hardware key)
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Even after his password was stolen in a data breach, his bank account remained secure because the attacker could not provide the 2FA code from his authenticator app.

key_vocabulary.definition: A social engineering attack where an attacker impersonates a trusted entity to trick victims into revealing sensitive information, clicking malicious links, or performing harmful actions
key_vocabulary.example_usage: The phishing email looked exactly like a notification from her bank, but hovering over the link revealed it pointed to a completely different domain.

key_vocabulary.definition: An automated attack that uses username-password pairs leaked from one service breach to attempt login on many other services, exploiting password reuse
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Credential stuffing attacks affected 150,000 accounts after attackers used passwords leaked from a breached gaming site to access users' email accounts where they had reused the same password.

examples.problem: A person uses the password 'MyDog2019!' for their email, banking, social media, and shopping accounts. A shopping site gets breached and their password is leaked. Trace the potential damage.

steps.instruction: The breached shopping site's database is posted on hacker forums. The person's email and password are now public.
steps.result: Thousands of attackers now have the email-password combination.

steps.instruction: Credential stuffing: attackers automatically try this password on major email providers, banks, and social media platforms.
steps.result: Because the same password was used everywhere, the attacker gains access to email, banking, and social media.

steps.instruction: Email access enables everything: the attacker uses 'forgot password' on other services, receives the reset links in the compromised email, and takes over additional accounts the person may not even remember having.
steps.result: Email is the master key. Compromising email enables taking over virtually every other online account.

steps.instruction: With banking access, the attacker initiates transfers. With social media access, they impersonate the person to scam their contacts. With email access, they intercept any recovery attempts.
steps.result: Financial theft, identity theft, and social engineering of the person's contacts.

examples.answer: Reusing one password across accounts turns a single breach (shopping site) into total compromise (email, banking, social media, and all connected services). The fix: unique passwords for every account (via password manager) and 2FA on email and banking. This limits the damage of any single breach to only the breached service.

examples.problem: You receive this email: 'Dear Customer, We detected unusual activity on your account. Click here to verify your identity immediately or your account will be suspended within 24 hours. — Your Bank Security Team.' Evaluate whether this is legitimate.

steps.instruction: Check the sender address: the email is from 'security@yourbank-alerts.com' instead of 'security@yourbank.com'.
steps.result: The domain is slightly different from the real bank. This is a common phishing technique.

steps.instruction: Check the greeting: 'Dear Customer' is generic. Your real bank knows your name and would use it.
steps.result: Generic greetings suggest a mass phishing email, not a targeted communication from your actual bank.

steps.instruction: Check the urgency: '24 hours or suspension' creates pressure to act without thinking. Legitimate banks rarely impose such tight deadlines for account verification.
steps.result: Artificial urgency is a hallmark of social engineering designed to bypass critical thinking.

steps.instruction: Hover over the link (without clicking): the URL shows 'http://verify-account-secure.xyz/login' instead of your bank's actual website.
steps.result: The link goes to a fake website designed to capture your login credentials.

examples.answer: This is a phishing email. Red flags: slightly wrong sender domain, generic greeting, artificial urgency, and a link to a fake website. The correct response: do NOT click the link. If concerned about your account, open your browser separately and navigate directly to your bank's website by typing the URL yourself, or call the bank using the number on your bank card.

practice_problems.question: Rank the following passwords from weakest to strongest: (a) password123, (b) P@s5w0Rd!, (c) correct-horse-battery-staple, (d) 7j$kL2#mN9pQ. Explain your ranking.
hints: Consider length, predictability, and whether each appears in common password lists.
distractors: The one with the most special characters is strongest
practice_problems.answer: Weakest to strongest: (a) 'password123' (appears in every leaked password database, crackable instantly), (b) 'P@s5w0Rd!' (looks complex but is a common substitution pattern that password crackers account for, only 9 characters), (d) '7j$kL2#mN9pQ' (12 random characters, strong but hard to memorize), (c) 'correct-horse-battery-staple' (28 characters, enormous search space despite being made of common words, and memorable). Length is the primary factor: the 28-character passphrase has vastly more possible combinations than even the 12-character random string, and it is easier to remember.
practice_problems.explanation: The common belief that substituting letters with symbols (a->@, s->5) makes passwords strong is outdated. Password crackers include these substitution rules. Length and randomness are what matter. A long passphrase of random words is both stronger and more usable than a short string of random characters.

practice_problems.question: Why is SMS-based 2FA (codes sent by text message) better than no 2FA but worse than an authenticator app?
hints: Think about how SMS messages are delivered and what could go wrong.
distractors: SMS 2FA is just as secure as authenticator apps
practice_problems.answer: SMS 2FA is better than no 2FA because it requires the attacker to have both your password and access to your phone number, which blocks most automated credential stuffing attacks. However, it is weaker than authenticator apps because: (1) SIM swapping: an attacker can call your phone carrier, impersonate you, and transfer your number to their SIM card, intercepting your SMS codes. (2) SS7 vulnerabilities: the underlying phone network protocol has known flaws that allow message interception. (3) Social engineering: phone carrier employees can be tricked into redirecting numbers. (4) Malware: phone malware can read incoming SMS messages. Authenticator apps (TOTP) generate codes locally on your device without any network transmission, eliminating all four vulnerabilities. Hardware keys (FIDO2) are even more secure because they require physical possession and are immune to phishing.
practice_problems.explanation: The hierarchy of 2FA security is: no 2FA (worst) < SMS (better) < authenticator app (much better) < hardware key (best). Any 2FA is dramatically better than no 2FA; the choice between types matters mainly for high-value accounts (email, banking) where the consequences of compromise are severe.

practice_problems.question: A friend says 'I do not need a password manager because I have a system: I use the same base password with the site name added. For Facebook it is MyDog-Facebook, for Gmail it is MyDog-Gmail.' Evaluate this approach.
hints: What happens if one of these passwords is leaked? Can the attacker figure out the pattern?
distractors: This is a good system because each password is technically different
practice_problems.answer: This approach is significantly better than identical passwords but still has critical weaknesses: (1) Pattern vulnerability: if 'MyDog-Facebook' is leaked in a breach, any attacker can see the pattern and try 'MyDog-Gmail', 'MyDog-Bank', etc. Automated tools can detect and exploit these patterns. (2) Predictable structure: the base 'MyDog' is short and weak, and the site name is publicly known. (3) Limited character diversity: the predictable pattern reduces the effective search space compared to truly random passwords. (4) No protection against site-specific leaks of the base: if 'MyDog' is in the leaked data, every account is compromised. A password manager generates truly random, unique passwords with no exploitable pattern, and remembers them all. The 30 minutes spent setting one up eliminates this vulnerability permanently.
practice_problems.explanation: Pattern-based password systems give a false sense of security. They protect against the most basic credential stuffing (exact password reuse) but not against any attacker who examines the leaked password for a few seconds and recognizes the pattern.

misconceptions.misconception: Complex short passwords (like P@s5!) are stronger than long simple passwords (like purple-mountain-sunset-river).
misconceptions.correction: Length is more important than complexity. A 4-word passphrase with 27 characters has approximately 10^17 possible combinations if the attacker knows you are using 4 random dictionary words (from a 10,000-word dictionary). An 8-character complex password has approximately 10^15 combinations. The passphrase is 100 times stronger AND easier to remember. This is why modern security guidance (NIST SP 800-63B) recommends long passphrases over short complex passwords.
misconceptions.why_common: For decades, security advice emphasized complexity (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols). This was appropriate when password length limits were short (8 characters). Modern understanding recognizes that length provides more entropy per character than complexity, and longer passwords are more usable.

misconceptions.misconception: Changing your password frequently makes your account more secure.
misconceptions.correction: Forced frequent password changes (every 30-90 days) actually reduce security because people respond by choosing weaker, more predictable passwords (Summer2024! becomes Fall2024!). NIST's 2017 guidelines explicitly recommend AGAINST routine password expiration, instead recommending password changes only when there is evidence of compromise. A strong, unique password that you keep for years is more secure than a weak password you change monthly.
misconceptions.why_common: Mandatory password rotation was security best practice for decades and is still enforced by many organizations. The policy made sense when passwords were more likely to be compromised through shoulder surfing or written notes, but it is counterproductive in the modern threat landscape where the main risks are breaches and phishing.

challenge.scenario: Your school's IT system gets breached and the student database (including email addresses and passwords) is leaked online. You used the password 'Matric2026!' for your school account, and — like most people — the same password for your personal email, Instagram, and online banking.
challenge.task: Trace the chain of damage that could unfold from this single breach. Then evaluate which security measure would have prevented the cascade.

options.label: Credential stuffing
options.description: Attackers automatically try your leaked school password on Gmail, Instagram, banks — and it works everywhere

options.label: Email is the master key
options.description: With your email compromised, attackers use 'forgot password' to take over every other account

options.label: Unique passwords per site
options.description: If each account had a different password, the breach would be limited to just the school account

options.label: 2FA stops the cascade
options.description: Even with the correct password, 2FA on email and banking would block unauthorized access

options.label: Strong password prevents breaches
options.description: If your password had been more complex, the school database wouldn't have been breached

challenge.expected_misconception: Students focus on password strength (making it more complex) when the real vulnerability is password reuse. 'Matric2026!' is a reasonable password — the problem isn't its strength but the fact that it's the same everywhere. When a database is breached, even the strongest password is exposed in plaintext or easily cracked. No password complexity can protect against a server-side breach.
challenge.discovery_bridge: The password security equation has two sides: strength (how hard to guess) and uniqueness (how contained is the damage). Most people optimize for strength while ignoring uniqueness. A password manager solves both: it generates random, unique passwords for every account and remembers them all. Combined with 2FA on your email and bank, this turns a catastrophic cascade into a minor inconvenience limited to one account.

creation.prompt: Write a step-by-step action plan (3-4 sentences) for someone who just realized they use the same password for everything. What should they do first, second, and third to protect themselves right now?
creation.alt_prompt: Explain to a skeptical parent why a password manager is safer than their current system of 'memorizing different passwords' (which in practice means reusing 2-3 passwords). Write 3-4 convincing sentences.
